patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 42-year-old anxious but clear-speaking elementary school teacher. Start by volunteering: 2 weeks of worsening exhaustion, shortness of breath with one flight of stairs, yellow eyes/skin, tea-colored urine, and episodes of heart racing when you stand. Say you’re worried it’s hepatitis, COVID, dehydration, or iron deficiency.

If asked, add: dull ache/fullness under the left ribs, headaches/lightheadedness, looking pale, low-grade fevers, intermittent back pain, and decreased appetite.

Only reveal if specifically prompted about triggers/cold/meds: symptoms worsened after a recent cold/viral illness; cold sensitivity with numb/bluish fingers; mild chest tightness only when very winded that resolves with rest; normal periods; you started an antibiotic a few weeks ago.

Withhold unless asked directly and reassured about confidentiality/nonjudgment: online “immune booster”/herbal supplements, increased alcohol lately, and one-time cocaine use.

Express cost/time worries: ask to limit labs to “only once,” assume specialists/immune meds are unaffordable, and prefer evening/weekend care. If the clinician shows empathy, you become more forthcoming and ask practical options.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of Sinding-Larsen-Johansson disease (traction apophysitis at the inferior patella) without unnecessary treatment/referral actions.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "Sinding-Larsen-Johansson disease" and frames it as an overuse injury/traction apophysitis related to growth and repetitive stress.

Elicits key history consistent with SLJ: anterior knee pain localized to inferior pole of patella, worsened by running/jumping/squatting/kneeling, gradual onset, sports participation, recent increase in activity, and age consistent with adolescent growth.

Assesses functional impact and red flags: inability to bear weight, true locking, large effusion, acute traumatic mechanism, fever/systemic symptoms, nighttime pain, or rapidly worsening pain; if present, advises urgent evaluation rather than routine SLJ management.

Performs or describes a focused knee exam: point tenderness at inferior patellar pole/patellar tendon origin, pain with resisted knee extension, evaluation of swelling/effusion, range of motion, extensor mechanism integrity, and basic ligament/meniscus screen as appropriate.

Mentions reasonable differentials briefly (e.g., Osgood-Schlatter disease, patellar tendinopathy/jumper’s knee, patellofemoral pain syndrome, patellar sleeve fracture if acute trauma) and explains why SLJ fits best in this presentation.

Explains initial management in plain language even if not prescribing: activity modification/relative rest, ice, stretching/strengthening (quadriceps/hamstrings), gradual return to sport, and that symptoms are typically self-limited with growth.

Meets communication milestones: explains diagnosis including the exact phrases "Sinding-Larsen-Johansson disease" and "overuse injury"; explains treatment including "activity modification" and "NSAIDs" as an option for short-term pain control.

If discussing NSAIDs, performs required safety checks in conversation before any prescribing: asks about medication allergies (especially NSAID/aspirin), contraindications (history of GI ulcer/bleed, kidney disease, anticoagulant use, asthma with NSAID sensitivity), and (non-critical) drug interactions; avoids recommending NSAIDs if contraindications/allergy are present.

Provides appropriate reassurance and anticipatory guidance: expected course, importance of avoiding pain-provoking activity, and warning signs that should prompt re-evaluation (worsening pain, swelling/effusion, inability to extend knee, persistent symptoms despite rest).

Avoids inappropriate claims (e.g., calling it infection, rheumatoid disease, or requiring surgery routinely) and avoids unnecessary imaging for classic presentations unless atypical features or trauma are present.

allergies: Succinylcholine (reported severe hyperkalemia after anesthesia; avoid depolarizing neuromuscular blockers)
